text: The patient is positioned for surgery, with the assistant holding the vascular pedicle taut. The right-handed surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, using scissors to split the peritoneum and expose the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, while maintaining traction on the pedicle.

The patient is positioned in a steep head-down orientation. The surgeon identifies the duodenum and uses it as a landmark to guide dissection toward the origin of the vascular pedicle. The dissection plane is parallel to the aorta, splitting the peritoneum. The pedicle is lifted to reveal loose areolar tissue between the superior hypogastric pre-aortic plexus and the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The surgeon delineates the origin of the IMA and creates a window underneath the pedicle of the IMA and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), aiding in the identification of the ureter before transecting the vascular pedicle.

The surgeon uses plastic clips or a vascular stapler to divide the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Following this, dissection proceeds from medial to lateral towards the fascia of Toldt, focusing on the mesocolon of the retroperitoneal structure. Attention is then diverted to the top of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, where traction and counter traction are applied to ensure entry into the correct TME plane for a bloodless dissection.

The surgeon dissects the posterior TME plane, then proceeds to split the anterior peritoneum while preserving the hypogastric, inferior epigastric, and superior epigastric plexus. A large loop of redundant sigmoid colon is mobilized early to clear the pelvic view, facilitating further rectal dissection.

The surgeon uses a fan retractor to facilitate low pelvic dissection, aiming to achieve a dissection margin four to five centimeters below the rectal tumor, approximately six centimeters from the anal verge.

The surgeon demonstrates the right hypogastric nerve emerging from the superior hypogastric plexus. The bilobed appearance of the TME plane is visible, indicating correct dissection. The assistant pulls the rectum out of the pelvic cavity, providing a clear view of the lower TME planes for continued dissection under tension. The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization from right to left, facilitating the opening of the left to right plane.

The surgeon performs a low anterior dissection, dividing the mesorectum approximately six centimeters from the anal verge using an energy device. The dissection is carried out without extending to the pelvic floor, utilizing the natural tapering of the TME plane.

The surgeon, using a right-handed approach, divides the right side of the mesorectum and performs posterior and anterior dissection before proceeding with the left-sided division at the anastomosis site. The left colon is mobilized up to the splenic flexure, revealing the mesocolon fully separated from the retroperitoneal structure. The surgeon ensures complete mobilization for maximum anastomotic mobility and checks the length of the proximal left colon after placing the anvil of the stapling device.

After transecting the rectum during the low anterior resection, the rectal stump visibly springs back towards the pelvic floor. The surgeon checks for any twist in the bowel and performs a leak test, which shows satisfactory results.
